verses.text: Elihu calls Job to hear, declaring, 'the spirit of God hath made me.' He speaks as a fellow creature, not an oppressor. Those who would correct others do well to remember they too are but the work of God's hands.

Elihu calls Job to hear, declaring, 'the spirit of God hath made me.' He speaks as a fellow creature, not an oppressor. Those who would correct others do well to remember they too are but the work of God's hands.

Elihu calls Job to hear, declaring, 'the spirit of God hath made me.' He speaks as a fellow creature, not an oppressor. Those who would correct others do well to remember they too are but the work of God's hands.

Elihu calls Job to hear, declaring, 'the spirit of God hath made me.' He speaks as a fellow creature, not an oppressor. Those who would correct others do well to remember they too are but the work of God's hands.

He invites Job to answer, for he is Job's equal, formed of the same clay; his terror will not crush him. He offers debate without intimidation. Truth is best served when the strong do not overawe the weak.

He invites Job to answer, for he is Job's equal, formed of the same clay; his terror will not crush him. He offers debate without intimidation. Truth is best served when the strong do not overawe the weak.

He invites Job to answer, for he is Job's equal, formed of the same clay; his terror will not crush him. He offers debate without intimidation. Truth is best served when the strong do not overawe the weak.
Elihu recalls Job's words: that he is clean, yet God finds occasions against him and counts him an enemy. He names the heart of Job's complaint. It is fair to state plainly what we mean to answer.
Elihu recalls Job's words: that he is clean, yet God finds occasions against him and counts him an enemy. He names the heart of Job's complaint. It is fair to state plainly what we mean to answer.
Elihu recalls Job's words: that he is clean, yet God finds occasions against him and counts him an enemy. He names the heart of Job's complaint. It is fair to state plainly what we mean to answer.
Elihu recalls Job's words: that he is clean, yet God finds occasions against him and counts him an enemy. He names the heart of Job's complaint. It is fair to state plainly what we mean to answer.

'In this thou art not just,' Elihu says; why strive with God, who gives no account of His matters? He gently rebukes Job's boldness. The creature has no standing to demand that the Creator explain Himself.

'In this thou art not just,' Elihu says; why strive with God, who gives no account of His matters? He gently rebukes Job's boldness. The creature has no standing to demand that the Creator explain Himself.

God speaks, Elihu says, once and twice — in dreams and night visions — to turn man from sin and hide pride from him. God warns before He strikes. His whispers in the night are mercies meant to keep us from the pit.

God speaks, Elihu says, once and twice — in dreams and night visions — to turn man from sin and hide pride from him. God warns before He strikes. His whispers in the night are mercies meant to keep us from the pit.

He also speaks through pain on the sickbed, when the body wastes and the soul draws near the grave. Affliction is another of God's voices. He uses suffering to arrest the soul that would not heed gentler calls.

He also speaks through pain on the sickbed, when the body wastes and the soul draws near the grave. Affliction is another of God's voices. He uses suffering to arrest the soul that would not heed gentler calls.

He also speaks through pain on the sickbed, when the body wastes and the soul draws near the grave. Affliction is another of God's voices. He uses suffering to arrest the soul that would not heed gentler calls.

If a messenger, an interpreter, shows the man his uprightness, God is gracious: 'Deliver him from going down to the pit: I have found a ransom.' Here shines the gospel. God Himself provides the ransom that delivers the dying sinner — fulfilled in Christ.

If a messenger, an interpreter, shows the man his uprightness, God is gracious: 'Deliver him from going down to the pit: I have found a ransom.' Here shines the gospel. God Himself provides the ransom that delivers the dying sinner — fulfilled in Christ.

If a messenger, an interpreter, shows the man his uprightness, God is gracious: 'Deliver him from going down to the pit: I have found a ransom.' Here shines the gospel. God Himself provides the ransom that delivers the dying sinner — fulfilled in Christ.

Then the man prays, sees God's favour, and is restored with joy, his soul delivered from the pit to see the light. Grace turns the sickbed into a place of mercy. The God who wounds also heals the penitent who calls on Him.

Then the man prays, sees God's favour, and is restored with joy, his soul delivered from the pit to see the light. Grace turns the sickbed into a place of mercy. The God who wounds also heals the penitent who calls on Him.

Then the man prays, sees God's favour, and is restored with joy, his soul delivered from the pit to see the light. Grace turns the sickbed into a place of mercy. The God who wounds also heals the penitent who calls on Him.

All this, Elihu says, God works again and again, to bring back the soul and enlighten it with the light of the living. His dealings are patient and repeated. He does not weary of seeking to save the wandering.

All this, Elihu says, God works again and again, to bring back the soul and enlighten it with the light of the living. His dealings are patient and repeated. He does not weary of seeking to save the wandering.
Elihu bids Job listen and learn, but invites him to answer if he can, 'for I desire to justify thee.' He seeks Job's good, not his defeat. Honest correction aims to restore, not merely to win.
